text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes a dilated stomach and duodenum with a high-riding cecum in the right upper quadrant. Adhesions between the right colon and lateral abdominal wall are lysed using countertraction and thermal energy. The colon is reflected medially to expose the duodenum and pylorus. The duodenum is mobilized, and a partial Kocher maneuver is performed, revealing its abnormal position on the right side of the abdominal cavity. Adhesions between the colon and duodenum are lysed using sharp dissection and a harmonic scalpel. Ladd's bands are identified in the right upper quadrant, adhering the cecum to the abdominal wall. The small bowel is examined distally, and adhesions are freed, including a thick fibrous band between the small bowel and mesentery, which is lysed with sharp dissection. This band is identified as the cause of the obstruction. After adhesiolysis, the duodenum and jejunum appear unobstructed, and the mesentery is broadened.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy by ligating the mesoappendix with a harmonic scalpel and removing the appendix using two endo loops. The appendiceal stump is cauterized and closed with a PDS suture. Following the appendectomy, the cecum is positioned in the left lower quadrant, while the duodenum remains on the right side of the abdominal cavity.
